perturbation.original_text: This Non -Disclosure Agreement shall expire on three (3) calendar years from December 31 of the year in which you execute this No n-Disclosure Agreement; provided, however, that notwithstanding the termination of this Non -Disclosure Agreement, the confidentiality obligations and covenants of this Non -Disclosure Agreement will survive indefinitely and shall not terminate, or else shal l remain in effect as long as permitted by law.
perturbation.changed_text: This Non -Disclosure Agreement shall expire on one (1) calendar year from December 31 of the year in which you execute this No n-Disclosure Agreement; provided, however, that notwithstanding the termination of this Non -Disclosure Agreement, the confidentiality obligations and covenants of this Non -Disclosure Agreement will terminate immediately.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the NDA term to one year and eliminating the survival of confidentiality obligations could conflict with the reasonable duration required to protect trade secrets under Georgia law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Georgia Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Official Code of Georgia Annotated (O.C.G.A.) § 10-1-760 et seq.)
perturbation.law_explanation: Georgia's UTSA protects trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. Eliminating confidentiality obligations immediately after the NDA expires may not provide sufficient protection for GTC's trade secrets, potentially conflicting with their rights under the UTSA to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Lexis® - Sign In | LexisNexis You did not enter an ID value. You did not enter a Password value. ID Please enter a valid ID. Password Please enter a valid password. Use of this service is subject to terms & conditions. Terms of use Your use of this service is subject to the LexisNexis ® Terms & Conditions. You must accept these Terms & Conditions to use the service. Please also review the Privacy & Cookies policy
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the NDA term to one year and eliminates the survival of confidentiality obligations, while the law explanation states that Georgia's UTSA protects trade secrets for as long as they remain secret. The perturbation explanation also suggests this conflicts with the reasonable duration required to protect trade secrets under Georgia law. Since the changed text eliminates confidentiality obligations immediately after the NDA expires, this contradicts the requirement for sufficient protection of trade secrets, especially considering the intent of the UTSA. The provided snippets do not directly support this, but the explanations do.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient may disclose the Confidential Information solely to : (a) those individuals within your organization who (i ) have requested access to Confidential Information from GTC and have been granted such access; (ii) need to know the information to do their jobs; (iii) have been advised of the duty of non - disclosure; and (iv) have executed this Non-Disclosure Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient may disclose the Confidential Information solely to : (a) any individuals within your organization who need to know the information to do their jobs, regardless of whether they have executed this Non-Disclosure Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing disclosure to individuals who haven't signed the NDA contradicts the requirement for reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, potentially violating Georgia law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Georgia Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Official Code of Georgia Annotated (O.C.G.A.) § 10-1-761(4))
perturbation.law_explanation: The Georgia UTSA defines a trade secret, in part, as information that is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. Disclosing confidential information to individuals who have not signed an NDA could be deemed a failure to take reasonable measures to maintain secrecy, potentially jeopardizing trade secret protection.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for individuals to have signed a Non-Disclosure Agreement (NDA) to access confidential information, which directly contradicts the goal of maintaining secrecy. The perturbation explanation states that this change potentially violates Georgia law because it undermines reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. The law explanation supports this, arguing that disclosing confidential information to those who haven't signed an NDA could be seen as a failure to take reasonable measures to maintain secrecy. The original text required all individuals accessing confidential information to have executed the NDA, providing a clear safeguard. The changed text eliminates this safeguard, making the contradiction explicit and impactful. There are no scraped law snippets.

perturbation.original_text: GTC may revoke your access to Co nfidential Information at any time, with or without notice.
perturbation.changed_text: GTC may revoke your access to Confidential Information for any reason, with or without notice, and shall not be liable for any damages resulting from such revocation.
perturbation.explanation: While GTC can revoke access, disclaiming all liability could be problematic under Georgia law if the revocation causes damages that are a direct result of GTC's actions, especially if the actions are deemed negligent or in bad faith.
perturbation.law_explanation: O.C.G.A. § 13-8-2(a) states that a party generally cannot contract away liability for its own fraud or willful misconduct. While the modified clause doesn't explicitly permit fraud or willful misconduct, a court may interpret the broad disclaimer of liability as an attempt to circumvent this principle, especially if GTC's revocation is deemed to be malicious or in bad faith, causing direct damages to the Recipient.

Caps Lock is on Having Caps Lock on may cause you to enter your password incorrectly. Press Caps Lock to turn it off before entering your password. SHOW Remember Me Forgot your ID or password? Use of this service is subject to terms & conditions. Terms of use Your use of this service is subject to the LexisNexis ® Terms & Conditions. You must accept these Terms & Conditions to use the service. Please also review the Privacy & Cookies policy
perturbation.justification: The modified text attempts to disclaim liability for any damages resulting from the revocation of access to Confidential Information. The Law Explanation states that O.C.G.A. § 13-8-2(a) generally prohibits contracting away liability for one's own fraud or willful misconduct. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a login page to LexisNexis, the Law Explanation suggests that a court might view the broad disclaimer of liability as an attempt to circumvent O.C.G.A. § 13-8-2(a), especially if GTC's revocation is malicious or in bad faith. This creates a legal contradiction because the contract change could be interpreted as an attempt to remove liability for actions that Georgia law does not allow to be exempted from liability.
